content.problemStatement:
Switchin' the positions for you 

Cookin' in the kitchen, and I'm in the bedroom 

I'm in the Olympics, way I'm jumpin' through hoops 

Know my love infinite, nothin' I wouldn't do 

That I won't do, switchin' for you

Chef has an array AAA consisting of NNN integers.

Chef can swap two elements on positions iii and jjj (1‚â§i<j‚â§N)(1\le i\lt j \le N)(1‚â§i<j‚â§N) only if:


AiA_iAi‚Äã &\&& Aj=0A_j = 0Aj‚Äã=0, where &\&& denotes the bitwise AND operator.
Chef wants to know the **lexicographically minimum** array that can be obtained using any (possibly zero) number of given swaps.

Note: An array XXX of size NNN is lexicographically smaller than an array YYY of size NNN if Xi<YiX_i \lt Y_iXi‚Äã<Yi‚Äã, where iii is the smallest index on which XXX and YYY differ.


Input Format

The first line of input consists of an integer NNN - denoting the size of array AAA.
The second line of input consists of NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{N}A1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã - denoting array AAA.


Output Format
Output the lexicographically minimum array that can be obtained using any (possibly zero) number of given swaps.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The lexicographically minimum array that can be obtained is [1,2,5][1, 2, 5][1,2,5].
It can be obtained by swapping A2A_2A2‚Äã and A3A_3A3‚Äã since A2A_2A2‚Äã &\&& A3=0A_3 = 0A3‚Äã=0.

Explanation:
The lexicographically minimum array that can be obtained is [2,6,2][2, 6, 2][2,6,2]. No swaps can be made in the given array.

Explanation:
The lexicographically minimum array that can be obtained is [7,8,9,16][7, 8, 9, 16][7,8,9,16].
The swaps required to obtain [7,8,9,16][7, 8, 9, 16][7,8,9,16] are:

A1A_1A1‚Äã and A2A_2A2‚Äã since A1A_1A1‚Äã &\&& A2=0A_2 = 0A2‚Äã=0. The array becomes [7,8,16,9][7, 8, 16, 9][7,8,16,9].
A3A_3A3‚Äã and A4A_4A4‚Äã since A3A_3A3‚Äã &\&& A4=0A_4 = 0A4‚Äã=0. The array becomes [7,8,9,16][7, 8, 9, 16][7,8,9,16].
